# Screening Criteria & Business Model Analysis

## Part A — Initial Screening

### 1. Revenue Growth (10-Year Trend)

Look up the last 10 years of annual revenue from:
- SEC EDGAR (10-K filings): https://www.sec.gov/cgi-bin/browse-edgar
- Macrotrends: https://www.macrotrends.net
- Wisesheets / Stockanalysis.com

Classify the trend:
| Pattern | Interpretation |
|---------|----------------|
| Consistent 10%+ annual growth | Strong growth compounder |
| Growth slowing but still positive | Maturing business — assess moat |
| Flat or volatile | Cyclical or commoditised — higher risk |
| Declining | Structural headwinds — investigate cause |

**Scoring guide:**
- 5 — Consistent double-digit revenue growth over 10 years
- 4 — Solid growth (7–10% CAGR) with minor dips
- 3 — Moderate growth (3–6%) or recovering from a dip
- 2 — Flat or low growth (<3%) with no clear catalyst
- 1 — Declining revenues for 2+ consecutive years

---

### 2. Profitability

Check:
- Net income: positive for at least 3 of the last 5 years
- Gross margin trend: expanding, stable, or compressing?
- Operating leverage: is margin improving as revenue grows?

**Scoring guide:**
- 5 — Consistently profitable, expanding margins
- 4 — Profitable with stable margins
- 3 — Profitable but thin or volatile margins
- 2 — Break-even or recently turned profitable
- 1 — Consistently loss-making with no clear path to profitability

---

### 3. Market Capitalisation Gate

- < $1B → Micro-cap / small-cap: high risk, low institutional coverage
- $1B–$10B → Mid-cap: growing institutional interest
- > $10B → Large-cap: preferred for core positions

Note this as context, not a hard disqualifier.

---

### 4. Net Debt

Calculate: **Net Debt = Total Debt − Cash & Equivalents**

| Net Debt / EBITDA | Interpretation |
|-------------------|----------------|
| < 1× | Very conservative, financial flexibility |
| 1–2× | Healthy leverage |
| 2–3× | Manageable — monitor trends |
| 3–4× | Elevated — scrutinise cash generation |
| > 4× | High risk, especially in rising rate environment |

**Scoring guide:**
- 5 — Net cash position (negative net debt)
- 4 — Net debt < 1× EBITDA
- 3 — Net debt 1–2× EBITDA
- 2 — Net debt 2–4× EBITDA
- 1 — Net debt > 4× EBITDA or deteriorating trend

---

### 5. Institutional & Notable Investor Ownership

Sources:
- SEC 13F filings: https://www.sec.gov/cgi-bin/browse-edgar (search "13F")
- WhaleWisdom: https://whalewisdom.com
- Dataroma (Superinvestors): https://www.dataroma.com
- TIKR.com

Look for:
- **Increasing** institutional ownership over last 4 quarters
- High-conviction managers (Buffett, Ackman, Tepper, Einhorn, etc.) holding or building positions
- Hedge fund consensus — are multiple respected funds converging on this name?
- Passive (index) vs. active ownership split

**Scoring guide:**
- 5 — Multiple high-quality active managers building positions; rising institutional %
- 4 — Solid institutional ownership, stable or increasing
- 3 — Moderate institutional ownership, mixed signals
- 2 — Low institutional ownership or notable selling by quality investors
- 1 — Institutions exiting en masse; no notable investors present

---

## Part B — Business Model Analysis

### How Does the Company Earn Its Money?

#### Revenue Segments

From the 10-K or annual report, break down revenue by segment:
- What are the named segments?
- What % of revenue does each contribute?
- Which segments are growing vs. declining?
- Are segments diversified or concentrated (>70% in one segment = concentration risk)?

#### Revenue Characteristics

| Type | Description | Quality Signal |
|------|-------------|----------------|
| Recurring / SaaS | Subscription, licence, maintenance | High — predictable |
| Contracted | Long-term supply or service agreements | High — visibility |
| Transactional | One-off purchases | Medium — volume dependent |
| Spot / commodity | Market-priced, no contracts | Low — volatile |

**Scoring guide:**
- 5 — Predominantly recurring or contracted, diversified segments
- 4 — Mixed but majority high-quality recurring
- 3 — Balanced recurring/transactional
- 2 — Mostly transactional, concentrated in one segment
- 1 — Spot-priced or single-segment commodity

---

#### Geographic Diversification

From the 10-K geographic breakdown:
- Is revenue concentrated in one country / region?
- Does the company operate in high-growth emerging markets?
- Currency and geopolitical risk?

**Flag if > 70% of revenue from a single geography.**

---

#### Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)

Each industry has sector-specific KPIs. Common examples:

| Sector | Key KPIs |
|--------|----------|
| SaaS / Tech | ARR, NRR, CAC, LTV, churn rate, DAU/MAU |
| Retail | Same-store sales growth, inventory turns, GMV |
| Banking / Fintech | NIM, NPL ratio, cost-to-income ratio, CET1 |
| Healthcare | Pipeline breadth, approval rates, patent cliff dates |
| Industrial | Capacity utilisation, backlog, book-to-bill ratio |
| Consumer | Brand NPS, repeat purchase rate, market share |

Find KPI disclosures in:
- Earnings call transcripts (Seeking Alpha, company IR)
- Investor Day presentations
- Supplemental data packages on the IR page

**Scoring guide (Business Model overall):**
- 5 — Clear, differentiated model; recurring revenue; diversified geographically; strong KPI trajectory
- 4 — Good model clarity; mostly recurring; manageable geographic concentration
- 3 — Understandable model; mixed revenue quality; some KPI concerns
- 2 — Unclear or complex model; predominantly transactional; deteriorating KPIs
- 1 — Opaque or rapidly changing model; no clear competitive differentiation
